source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 4.5.8-4.5.9 sort and wc (pp. 54-57)

questions.q: Sort the lines of the file colours.txt and print them in order.
questions.why: sort orders the lines of a file on screen; the file itself is not modified.

questions.q: Why does sort need the -n option for numeric data?
options: Without it, numbers are sorted as characters, so '110' comes before '20'
Without it, sort refuses to run on numbers
questions.why: By default sort compares character by character, so '110' precedes '20'; -n compares the blocks by numeric value.

questions.q: Which sort option reverses the order of the result (e.g. largest first when combined with -n)?
questions.why: -r reverses the order; combined with -n it sorts from largest to smallest.

questions.q: Sort colours.txt and remove duplicate lines in one command.
questions.why: -u removes duplicates after sorting - equivalent to piping 'sort FILE | uniq'.

questions.q: Sort the file size.txt by human-readable file sizes (like 1.7G, 18M, 69K), largest first.
questions.why: -h understands the human-readable size suffixes produced by 'ls -h'; -r puts the largest first.

questions.q: The plain 'sort file' does not change the file. How do you save the sorted result?
options: Use -o file, or redirect the output with >
sort always overwrites the file in place
You cannot save a sorted result
questions.why: sort only prints to the screen; -o writes to a named file, as does an output redirection with >.

questions.q: Count the number of lines in the file .bash_profile.
questions.why: wc -l counts lines; wc alone reports lines, words and bytes together.

questions.q: In wc, which option counts words and which counts bytes?
